convo.text:
```js
function dedup(a){ return [...new Set(a)]; }
```

This drops you from **O(n²) to O(n)**. The original calls `a.indexOf(x)` for every element, and each `indexOf` scans the array — so n elements × n-length scans = n² comparisons. A `Set` does insertion and membership checks in amortized O(1), so building it and spreading back to an array is a single linear pass.

Both versions preserve first-occurrence order and behave identically on primitives. One semantic difference to know: `Set` treats `NaN` as equal to `NaN` (so duplicate `NaN`s collapse correctly), whereas the `indexOf` version never dedupes `NaN` because `indexOf` uses strict equality and `NaN !== NaN`. The Set behavior is almost always what you actually want.

Rough impact: at n=10,000 you go from ~100M comparisons to ~10K operations — typically a few hundredfold faster, and the gap widens as the array grows.
